---
title: "data.frame stuff in dplyr vs. pandas"
description: "r & python"
draft: true
---

### load data

::: panel-tabset

### dplyr

```{r, message=FALSE}
library(dplyr)
# subset a few columns for simplicity
sw <- starwars[, 1:7]
```

### pandas
```{python}
import pandas as pd
# this imports the sw dataset from the R code chunk
sw = r.sw
```
:::

### Subset rows and columns by index

::: panel-tabset

### dplyr

`slice` rows by index, `select` columns by index or name.

```{r, message=FALSE}
sw %>% 
      # rows
      slice(1:3) %>% 
      # columns
      select(1:3)
```

### pandas

`.iloc`, think *integer location* to subset rows and columns. For subsetting
by name, use `loc`.

```{python}
(sw   # rows
      .iloc[0:3]
       # columns
      .iloc[:, 0:3])
```

:::

### Subset rows by condition

::: panel-tabset

### dplyr
```{r, message=FALSE}
sw %>% 
      filter((mass > 100) & (height > 200))
```

### pandas
```{python}
sw[(sw.mass > 100) & (sw.height > 200)]
```

:::

### Group by and summarise

::: panel-tabset

### dplyr
```{r, message=FALSE}
sw %>% 
      group_by(eye_color) %>% 
      summarise(mass_median = median(mass, na.rm=TRUE),
                heigh_median = median(height, na.rm=TRUE))
```

### pandas
```{python}
(sw
      .groupby('eye_color')
      .agg({'mass': 'median', 'height': 'median'}))
```

:::
